                                                       VOL. SI NO. 6 DECEMBER 1974

                Joseph M. Finerty, Editor               Gloria P. Green, Associate Editor            James A. McCall, Associate Editor


                                                              Subscription price increase
                          Beginning December 1974, the cost of subscribing to Employment and Earnings has been raised to
                       $37.20 a year. The additional cost for mailing to foreign countries is $9.30 a year. Single copies cost
                       $3.10 each.
                          The Government Printing Office, which handles printing and distribution of Employment and Earnings
                       and other U.S. Government publications, has placed the higher prices into effect.
                          The Bureau of Labor Statistics of the Department of Labor, which publishes Employment and Earnings
                       and pays research, editorial, and production costs out of its budget, has no voice in setting subscription prices
                       and does not receive any of the sales revenue.

                 Beginning with this issue of Employment and Earn-             in activity which are centered in a few industries indicate
            ings a new set of series—diffusion indexes of changes             that there may be particular problems that would require
            in employment on nonagricultural payrolls—will be                  individualized attention. Widespread changes portend a
           published (see table B-7 p. 79). Two series of diffusion           general economic slowdown or expansion which would

                 A diffusion index is a measure of the breadth or dis-        as sudden dramatic shifts. Rather, some industries will
            persion of changes in some phenomenon, in these cases             begin to decline (or expand) well in advance of others.
            payroll employment. It may be interpreted as the percent          As a result, the diffusion index will hit its peak (or
           of industries showing employment increases. Thus, a                trough) before the declining (expanding) industries ac-
           diffusion index of 63.2 means that 63.2 percent of the             tually cause the total estimate to decline (expand). This
            industries posted increases and 36.8 percent posted               mechanism ties the diffusion index closely to the rate
           declines.                                                          of change in the aggregate series. As a result, a slowing
                 An index may be computed for various time spans.             in the rate of growth will usually induce a decline in
           Thus, an index over a 3-month span measures the dif-               the diffusion index. (For example, see the year 1966.)

                                                                                      MCD (months for cyclical dominance) provides an
                                                Median lead (-) or lag (+)              estimate of the average time span required for
                                                        in months
                      Index and span                                                    the cyclical movements in a series to exceed the
                                               Peaks     Troughs     Both                irregular movements. Specifically, the MCD mea-
                                                                                        sure represents the minimum number of months
                                                                                        needed for the average change (without regard to
           172 industries
                                                                                        sign) in the cyclical component to exceed the aver-
               1-month span                   -14           -4       -9
              3-month span                    -13           -4       -9                 age percentage change (without regard to sign) in
              6-month                           -8          -2       -5                 the irregular component. Thus, by taking into
              12-month span                     -7         +3        -2                 account the MCD, the analyst can distinguish
           30 industries                                                                with more confidence whether the changes in a
               1-month span                     -8         -6        -7
                                                                                        series are of purely irregular nature or whether
              6-month span                      -8   -     -2       '-5
                                                                                        they indicate a cyclical turn in the series.
                                                                                     These measures indicate that the indexes are quite

                  tAs noted on page; 7 ot the January 1972 issue of this magazine, the senes does not include earned pay withheld as a consequence of delays caused hy the wage freeze in effect between August 15
               and November 14, 1971, and by administrative procedures of the Pay Board These delays resulted from compliance with ihe Government's Economic Stabilization Program. Because these withheld
               payments were substantial and fell due under a significant number of now labor-management contracts in that industry,they affected a large proportion of SIC 372. The series including those pay-
               ments for September 1971 • March 197i are shown below.

                    Wages and salaries of employees plus employers' contributions for social insurance and private benefit plans. Data also include an estimate of wages, salaries, and supplemental payments for

                                                                                                                151                                                    ESTABLISHMENT DATA
                                                                                                       Seasonal Factors

                 The following tables present seasonal adjustment factors for all scries in the establishment section of this periodical. These factors were derived using data through August 1974. The raciors should
             be used with current estimates (adjusted to March 1973 benchmarks) for September 1974 through August 1975 or until the date of the next benchmark revision.
                 The seasonal movements are measured in order to adjust the data statistically for such recurring events as warm and cold weather, crop-growing cycles, holidays, vacations, regular industry model
             changeover periods, and the like. These movements are generally the largest single component of month-to-month changes h employment, hours, and labor turnover.                 The seasonal factors which
             follow enable the analyst lo remove these influences from the data in order to determine more basic trends.

            by industry division and major manufacturing group

                  The    household        and       establishment           data           supplement         one      Hours of work
           another, each providing significant types of information that the
            other    cannot        suitably       supply. Population                  characteristics, for                 The        household      survey    measures       hours   actually           worked
